hierarchy.lvl4: Option 3 : Forest inventory data.
hierarchy.lvl3: Empirical data for biomass growth curves.

records.content:
Some forest inventories contain data that allow you to infer tree or stand age and the biomass of your species of interest. For example, the 

National Forest Inventory of Canada proposes raster maps of several forest attributes Canada-wide, including pixel/stand forest age, and the biomass of each individual species that are considered in the forest inventory.

If you use each pixel from the maps of a single year as a point and plot a cloud of points on a biomass/time axis, you might get a good estimation of a growth curve, with a growth peak and a initial growth (the two most important parts in our case). This is, in essence, a 

“space-for-time” substitution, where you look at many different points in space that will be forests at different stages of growth, rather than looking at the long-term history of a few forests.

The big limitation of this data is that you often get one “stand age” or “pixel age” value per pixel, rather than a separate age value for the trees of a given species in the pixel. As such, in a pixel of 80 years, you cannot know if the biomass of species X comes from trees that are 80 years old, or if these trees are younger. But you can interpret the upper points of the cloud of points as “the maximum biomass that a species can reach for this stand age”, which is pretty close to the mark of what you’re looking for.

Another big limitation is that in most pixels (if not all), your species will be mixed with other species. This is especially true if you are calibrating a “companion” species (the type of tree species that is never abundant, but yet found in many forests). You can restrict the points of your data to only the pixels where more than 50% of the biomass comes from your species of interest; but you might not get enough points for some rarer species. In these case, you will probably need to develop a correction of biomass to try and have an estimate of the biomass that your species can reach when it composes 100% of the stand. You might do this by making a statistical model that attempts to predict the biomass of your species in a stand or pixel based on the age of the pixel, and the % of composition of the species. You might want to train your statistic model on only the top percentile of points (as you’re looking out for the most biomass your species can achieve for a given age).

This data is often generated through a combination of forest inventory sampling (aerial photography, etc.) and sample plot data (which are assignated to stand or pixels via assignation algorithms like the kNN method). As such, it often contains information from sample plots, but which has already been cleaned and dealt with.

The biomass data is often expressed in biomass/surface, making it easy to compare to LANDIS-II outputs.

This is the data I will be using here. From what I heard, other teams have been using such data in the past.

What is the spatial extent for which I should take growth data for the calibration ? And what climate data should I use depending on the origin of my growth data ?

See 

the introductory notebook about some important notions before beginning the calibration for detailed answers.

What data should I use to calibrate variants, like southern variants that are more adapted to heat ? And what variant I am calibrating for if I use this methodology ?

See 

the introductory notebook about some important notions before beginning the calibration for detailed answers.

records.content:
After investigation, and since my own use case is to calibrate PnET-Succession for several landscapes in Canada, I’ve chosen to go with option 3 (Forest inventory data). More specifically, I’ m going to use the NFI (National Forest Inventory of Canada).

See :

https://​nfi​.nfis​.org​/en/ for general information and visualisation of some of the maps

https://​open​.canada​.ca​/data​/en​/dataset​/ec9e2659​-1c29​-4ddb​-87a2​-6aced147a990 to download the maps

Beaudoin et al. (2018) for details about how the maps were generated.

Here, I’m using data from the most recent year (2011). I’m using the map /NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Structure_Stand_Age_v1.tif for the stand (pixel) ages; /NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Structure_Biomass_TotalLiveAboveGround_v1.tif for biomass of stands; and a series of rasters like /NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Species_Abie_Bal_v1.tif that indicate the propotion of /NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Structure_Biomass_TotalLiveAboveGround_v1.tif that corresponds to the species of interest (e.g. Abies balsamea in that case). All maps are currently in ./ReferencesAndData.

I’m also using several “mask” rasters to restrict or differenciate the pixel data from different provinces in Canada (e.g. quebecMask.tif, etc., all in ./ReferencesAndData).

Here, before we use this dataset, I quickly show how it looks like by focusing on the highest points of the point clouds that it produces. Focusing on these highest points will give us an idea of the “best growth” that the species can achieve through by looking at different pixels from the same year throughout all of Canada.

# We read data from the NFI for our free species, and plot the distribution of the points above 95% of the maximum value in
# each 3 years window (which allows us to keep the top points)
from functionsForCalibration import *
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


# We define the path to where the rasters are
species_path = "./ReferencesAndData/NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Species_Abie_Bal_v1.tif"
age_path = "./ReferencesAndData/NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Structure_Stand_Age_v1.tif"
biomass_path = "./ReferencesAndData/NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Structure_Biomass_TotalLiveAboveGround_v1.tif"
# We define the paths to the mask rasters for each province
dictOfMasks = {"NS":"./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/novaScotiaMask.tif",
              "QC":"./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/quebecMask.tif",
              "ON":"./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/ontarioMask.tif",
              "MT":"./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/manitobaMask.tif",
              "SK":"./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/saskatchewanMask.tif",
              "AB":"./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/albertaMask.tif",
              "BC":"./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/britishColumbiaMask.tif",}

plottingNFISpeciesUpperBiomassByProvince(species_path,
                                         age_path,
                                         biomass_path,
                                         "Abies balsamea",
                                         dictOfMasks)





As such, we can see that :

The NFI data shows some pretty clear growth patterns (initial growth, peak, senescence)

We see that the different provinces can have very different growth peaks and initial growth/senescence. Upon closer inspection (plotting again but only for points with at least 50% of biomass composed of Abies balsamea), it seems to mostly be due to the differences in the mixture/competition of the species in difference province. SK, AB, MT and BC don’t have many points where Abies balsamea is dominant. Meanwhile, NS, QC and ON have a lot of it.

Now, here are the same figures for the other species :

records.content:
Based on the NFI data shown and explored in the previous section, we now have to produce estimated growth curves based on this data that we will be able to use as a “target” for the first step of the calibration of PnET-Succession, where we will try to match the curves produced by PnET-Succession with the data.

Since the first calibration step is based on growth curves in “ideal” conditions, where the species we are calibrating have no competition for water and for light, we especially have to estimate the growth curves for monospecific stands, where the relative abundance of the species is 100%. Unfortunatly, pixels that contains only one species are very rare in the NFI rasters since the pixels are 250x250m, and also because in nature, monospecific stands can be quite rare for many species. Even worse, some species have a very low amount of pixels in which they have a high relative abundance, or even none at all. A good example here is Acer rubrum, the red maple, which has a low amount of points with relative abondance above 50% when compared to the two other species we are calibrating here, Abies balsamea and Picea mariana. I expect that many other tree species available in the NFI will have the same issue.

As such, I propose to use statistical modelling to use the information contained in the pixels we do have (where the relative abundance of any given species is below 100%) in order to generate growth curves for different level of abundances, and especially for a 100% abundance. I set up to use Generalized Additive Models, as they model non-linear effects of variables quite well, and because I expect the effect of the factors that will be used in this model (forest age and relative species abundance) will be non-linear.

In this approach, we will treat every pixel from the NFI raster as a single data point associated with 3 variables, one for the different raster : age of the pixel; biomass of the species X in the pixel; and relative abundance (in %) of species X in the pixel. By grouping these points into age windows, we can then use a GAM to predict the upper percentile of biomass of species X observed for a given age window, and for a given % relative abundance. As such, by predicting the upper percentile per age window, the GAM will predict how much maximal biomass species X can achieve for a given age, and a given % relative abundance.

Once the GAM is fitted, we make predictions for a 100% relative abundance, which is what we want. These predictions will not be perfect, as very few (if none) pixels with a 100% relative abundance exist, as said before; and as such, the model will be extrapolating outside of the data on which it has been fitted. But whatever the approach we use (i.e. using the FVS model, or using sample plot data), the problem will be the same : as monospecific stands are rarely observed in nature, any estimate that we’re trying to get here will be based on an extrapolation one way or another. This is the limitation of using a first calibration step in “ideal conditions”, although using this approach has some great advantages. For more about this, take a look at 

0​.1​.Some​_important​_notions​.ipynb where this question is talked about in details.

Having these GAMs fitted will thus allow us to produce growth curves predicted by the GAM for different level of % relative abundance for the species X, including the 100% curve that we are looking for.

records.content:
I’ve made some first attempts fitting some relatively simple GAMs on the data of the NFI for the three species I’m calibrating here. Unfortunatly, this didn’t go well : the growth curves produced by the GAM was quite non-sensical, and didn’t fit the points very well. I’m not an expert in statistics, but I expect that this was because the model being fitted on patterns within the data that I couldn’t see with my human eyes (as in my eyes, the growth pattern visible in the data was quite clear). Maybe it’s the overabundance of low-abundance pixels, and also the presence of some high-abundance pixels with relatively low biomass values and other outliers tended to create unexpected and absurd GAM behaviour.

I solved this issue by pre-processing the data (see figure below) to 1) reduce the number of points, 2) remove the outliers, and 3) force a monotonic increasing rule regarding the effect of abundance of the high percentiles of biomass. This pre-processing is done by defining windows of age and % relative abundance; in each of these windows, we gather all of the pixels/points that are inside of it (i.e. pixels with an age and a % relative abundance for species X that have the value for the window); and then, we compute the upper percentile of biomass for the pixels/points in this window. We repeat the process accross all age/% relative abondance windows possible (without superposing the windows).

Furthermore, the function can remove points that break the monotonic increasing tendancy of the effect of the abundance on the biomass; i.e. if a percentile for an abundance window of 80 is lower than the percentile of the previous abundance window (e.g. 75) for the same age window, we will not take it into account. This is done to clarify the tendancy of the effect of the abundance on the biomass to help the GAMs fit better on the data, using the assumption that points that do not follow this tendancy are deemed to be errors or extremes.

Here is an depiction of the procedure in visual form :

This pre-processing is highly customizible, allowing you to define the size of the windows, the percentile computed, or to exclude certain pixels; see the function createPercentilesBiomassDatasetsFromNFIData in functionsForCalibration.py . You can put the argument printPlot as True in order to see the resulting points datasets.

The function createPercentilesBiomassDatasetsFromNFIData will generate four points datasets that correspond to four different spatial extent where we take the data from the NFI : the study landscape we are calibrating for; the ecozones that directly intersect with the study landscapes; the same ecozones + the ones that surrounds them in Canada; and finally, all of the data from the NFI for all of Canada. This is done so that we can fit GAMs and produce estimated growth curves for these different spatial extents, in order to compare the curves we obtain. The goal will be to select a curve that seems as realistic as possible while trying to select data as close to the study landscape as possible, in order to avoid mixing a lot of intra-specific variability (different phenotypes, variants, etc.) from the species in our dataset. However, sometimes, the data from the study landscape will not be enough to produce realistic curves, because there is not enough data points for the species in it (especially points with enough % relative abundance for the species).

As such, the function getGamDiagnostics (used after createPercentilesBiomassDatasetsFromNFIData) will compute some diagnostic values for the 100% abundance curves produced by the GAMs fitted on each of these datasets, and then use a simple algorithm to determine the best curve, and export it to a .csv file where it can be used in the subsequent notebooks for the calibration. The selection is made by first determining which curves are considered “valid” according to three diagnostics : a pseudo R2 > 0.85; a curve shape that is truly concave; and a percentage of negative biomass values along the curve (for the entire age range of the curve) being inferior to 20%. Then, we simply select the first valid curve in the subset of points that is the smallest (i.e. closest to the study landscape), in order to reduce the intra-specific variability present in the subset of points on which our estimation is based.

records.content:
The most robust GAM structure I’ve found after many trials accross the data for the three species we’re calibrating here is the following. It’s a GAM structure that produces mostly relatistic curves (e.g. curves that are concave, with clearly defined growth peaks, and start near 0) accross all species and data subsets (e.g. study landscape, ecozone, surrounding ecozones, and Canada).

\text{High Biomass Percentile} = s(age, by(\% abundance), \text{with a concave constraint}) +  s(\% abundance, by(age), \text{with a concave constraint}) + s(age, \text{with a concave constraint}) + s(\% abundance, \text{with a concave constraint})

Here, the by statement indicates a simple interaction between the two factors (see 

the documentation of the pyGAM package). We can also use a more complex interaction using a tensor (te()), but all of my attempts using tensors resulted in curves that didn’t make sense. It seems that the tensor was too sensitive to missing data, and tended to dip quickly into a negative effect, leading to non-sensical curves.

records.content:
The article from 

Tompalski et al. 2025 proposes stand-level yield curves (e.g. evolution of the biomass through the aging of a stand) for stands dominated by a particular tree species (27 species available). The curves have been created using different sources of remote sensing and aerial laser data. Furthermore, these curves are develloped inside 150x150km tiles accross Canada, with one curve per tile (as long as enough data for a given species was available in the tile).

We can use the data from the article, which is freely available in a very useful R package (see 

ptompalski/RSYC), in order to assess the estimates we have obtained with the GAMs.

Warning

Keep in mind that the biomass curves produced by Tompalski et al. 2025 are made with a different methodology than what we’re doing here. In particular, their curves represent the biomass of a stand dominated by a given species; but they don’t seem to remove the biomass from other species that will be inside the stand with the dominating species. Here, thanks to the NFI relative abundance raster, we do isolate the biomass for a given species. As such, species that can be dominant but which are still often not “very dominant” (not a lot of % relative biomass) will have their biomass mixed in with other species in the curves of Tompalski et al. 2025. This makes it that the curves of Tompalski et al. 2025 will often have higher biomass peaks than what is computed here for a given species; especially for species which display a low relative abondance accross most of their pixel because they are often companion species, like Acer rubrum.

Also, the curves of Tompalski et al. 2025 often display no scenescence/decrease in biomass, because they focus on the stand level and not on the species in the stand. Meanwhile, or method based on the NFI data does track the scenescence of given species inside a stand more easily, since we can isolate the biomass from the species of interest.

The following function will use the dictionnary dictOfSelectedCurvesSubsets.json created in the previous steps, which contains the point subset that has been select for each species during the previous section. Then, it will retrieve the tiles from Tompalski et al. 2025 that intersect with the spatial extent of the subset (from RSYC_tiles.gpkg that is available on 

ptompalski/RSYC), and then will retrieve the maximum biomass observed accross all tiles (already extracted into a .csv file named species_max_biomass_TompalskiEtAl2025.csv using the R script extractingMaximalBiomassFromTompalskiEtAl2025.R; we had to use an R script R since 

ptompalski/RSYC is a R package).

Finally, the function displays a bar plot showing the difference for each species between the biomass peak we got with the GAMs versus the average peak across all tiles intersecting the spatial extent used for the GAM in the curves of Tompalski et al. 2025. The black bar on the top of the Tompalski et al. bar shows the minimum and maximum observed accross the model for all available tiles; it’s not the standard deviation.

from functionsForCalibration import *

compare_biomass_models(study_landscape_shp_path = "./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/ManawanTerritoryExtent.shp",
                       ecozones_shp_path = "./ReferencesAndData/SpatialBoundaries/canadianEcozones.shp",
                       Tompalski_tiles_gpkg_path = "./ReferencesAndData/Growth Curves Predictions/RSYC_tiles.gpkg")















As we can see, the maximum biomass observed in Tompalski et al. 2025 agrees quite well with the GAM for Abies balsamea  and Picea Mariana. However, the GAMs give a much lower estimates in our case for Acer rubrum. My own interpretation is that because the methodology of Tompalski et al. makes growth curves for “stands dominated by a given species”; as such, the biomass of their curves also contains the biomass of trees other than the dominant one. Here, we’re isolating the biomass of each species from the one of other species completely, thanks to the relative abundance raster of the NFI. Because Acer rubrum is most often a companion species with a relatively low dominance (even when it is dominant in a stand), I expect that this curves from Tompalski et al. are much higher because they tend to contain the biomass of other species, while ours focuses on Acer rubrum.

As such, I’ll keep going with what the GAMs have generated here.

The approach used here has a lot of advantages (replicable, using open-source and peer-reviewed datasets that are easily available, can be adapted to future datasets of the same format, etc.). But it also have important limitations. In particular, two things have to be kept in mind :

We are using a statistical model to exprapolate, which means that the estimates that we get here are nothing but estimates.

Data from the NFI is on 250x250m cells, and is generated through k-NN association with sample plot data; what this means is that there is a lot of averaging going on. As a consequence, if you compare the estimate of biomass in tons/ha obtained here with sample plot data, you will tend to see much higher maximum biomass measure from the sample plot data, since there a lot more extreme values than in the NFI data.

The most important thing in the end is to make sure that the estimates of growth in ideal conditions we got here represent the differences between species quite well : whatever the values themselves, the ranking of species in terms of their growth capacity should be similar to their biological reality. It’s hard to consider that when we mix it with the fact that there is an important intra-specific variability that can mix things up; but overall, if you see one species that is supposed to be not very competitive achieving a lot of biomass compared to another one that is know to be very competitive or to achieve a lot of biomass, you might want to take a look at your curves again, and maybe select a different spatial extent or dataset.

Still, I consider this approach to be quite solid, especially after I contacted several researchers to get their opinion on it.

If you find a better approach, know that the growth curves selected here are simply exported into .csv files (in the folder ReferencesAndData/Growth Curves Predictions/) which are then re-used in the subsequent notebooks for the calibration of PnET-Succession.

As such, you can implement you better approach by modifying this notebook, and export the curves your approach produces in a .csv file for each species (you can use the same names, e.g. Latin name_gam_predictions.csv). In that way, your approach will integrate nicely with the rest of the calibration, and you won’t have to change much things.

For the US, you might want to use this dataset instead of the NFI, which is quite similar : 

https://​data​.fs​.usda​.gov​/geodata​/rastergateway​/bigmap​/index​.php.

records.content:
What we will call here “Longevity” and “Max Longevity” are necessary to calibrate the senescence part of the growth curve for our species.

The difference between the two is this, per the team of Brian Sturtevant who explained their methodology to me :

Longevity is the rule-of-thumb, expert opinion longevity for a given tree species. This is the age at which it’s expected that a tree of a given species will die, in a give region. These are the number often reported in the litterature, and also used in the core species parameter file of LANDIS-II as its “Longevity” parameter for each tree species used.

Max Longevity is the real, empirical maximum age that a tree species can reach in the study area. It’s often not reported and difficult to find. A good way to find it is to look at sample plot data and find the oldest trees ever found for a given species (which can be difficult to find as the “age” data is often not directly available in sample plots data).

Brian Sturtevant and his team have used these two notions to calibrate the senescence part of the growth curves in ideal conditions as explained in 

​.​/6​.Monoculture​_ideal​_conditions​.py.  This part from 

this supplementary material of Reese et al. (2025) describes Brian’s methodology of explains it all :

We used an unpublished review and analysis led by John Du Plissis at the University of Minnesota-Duluth to define both tree species longevity (the age at which a species age cohort can no longer persist) and the initiation of decline that is defined by the age-related photosynthesis reduction factor (PsnAgeRed) parameter in PnET Succession. In general, the maximum species longevity of Loehle (1988) corresponds with the tree species longevity parameter, while the typical species’ longevity from the same publication determines the initiation of decline, where PsnAgeRed is calibrated such that the fAGE (age reduction factor) begins to decline (~0.99) at the Typical Mortality age of the species.

The data used by Brian’s team for Typical Longevity and Max Longevity are cited as coming from 

Loehle (1988) in ReeseEtAl_CanJForRes_2024_supplA.docx ; it’s an article that seem to compute them based on several characteristics of tree species.

In the end, Brian’s team is using a combination of data from 

Loehle (1988) and estimates found by John Du Plissis (yet unpublished). I’m going to use the data from 

Loehle (1988), which should be enough for our goals until the data from Du Plissis is published.

Loehle (1988), defines “Typical” versus “Maximum” longevity in the following terms :

Typical longevity is the age of the older trees in an old-growth stand, whereas maximum longevity is the upper known maximum above which very few trees are found.

We can find the value of typical longevity and maximum longevity from the Appendix 1 of 

Loehle (1988) (see end of the .pdf). Here are the values for our three species :

records.content:
Table with ranges extracted from the litterature for most PnET parameters ?

From Brian’s  team (ReeseEtAl_CanJForRes_2024_supplA.docx) :

Parameter calibrations were guided by plausible ranges for ecophysiological parameter values and species traits from the TRY Plant Trait Database (

https://​www​.try​-db​.org​/TryWeb​/Home​.php), cross-referenced against region-specific species ecology from the Fire Effects Information System (

https://​www​.feis​-crs​.org​/feis/).

From the user guide :

The range of FolN for each species across sites, age, canopy position. This is a great starting point and helps put bounds on acceptable values, but it is important to recognize that productivity is closely related to FolN in PnET, so it is more important that FolN reflect relative productivity among species than matching empirical values. That said, it is usually (but not always) possible to select a value of FolN that is within the range of empirically measured values. If you have empirical values of Amax, use your values of AmaxA and AmaxB (or see Fig. 2 in Aber et al. 1996) to estimate the FolN that produces that Amax value. NOTE: if you plan to implement dynamic FolN (FolNInt and FolNSlope set to values other than default), then be sure to set FolNInt and FolNSlope to the values you will use in your final simulations while calibrating.

Species SLWmax helps determine LAI. Thicker leaves (higher SLW) reduce LAI. LAI determines the ability of a cohort to compete for light, so it is important to calibrate so that LAI produces realistic response to light. Again, it is more important to get LAI approximately right than to keep SLWmax within the empirical range. Out-of-range SLWmax values often indicate that FracFol values are incorrect. Note that higher LAI increases within-canopy competition for light and will modestly depress growth even of a single cohort. I have found that LAI values exceeding those given in Table 1 may greatly reduce the amount of light penetrating canopy layers, thus impacting growth, competition and establishment

First, I’m going to see if I’m able to find a TRY database measurement for most of the parameters of PnET-Succession. See the following table of species parameters in PnET :

Name

Necessary

Description

Additional notes

Found in TRY ?

FolN

Yes

Foliar nitrogen content (in % by weight) for the leaves of the species.

Value must be decimal, between 0 and 10. Unit is percentage. One of the most important parameters according to the sensitivity analysis of Ameray et al. (2023). Ameray et al. (2023) use values from Paré et al. (2013) which, according to them, work well for many species in the boreal forest.

Yes : Leaf nitrogen (N) content per leaf dry mass (14)

SLWmax

Yes

Specific leaf weight at the top of the canopy.

Must be decimal, between 0 and 1000, in g/m2. Values < 40 may result in extremely high LAI in lower canopy sublayers when SLWDel (the rate of change in specific leaf weight per subalyer) is > 0. The logic is that the leaves at the top of a tree (top of canopy) are most often smaller and waxier than the leaves under the canopy, to adapt to the fact that they receive a lot of light and transpirate easily. Leaves under the canopy are often larger (and thus weight more).

Yes

SLWDel

Yes

Rate of change in the weight of the leaves from the top of the canopy (SLWmax) to each sublayer under the canopy.

Must be decimal, between 0.0 and 2. Units are in proportion of SLWmax (see above). If SLWDel > 1, the leaves in subsequent subcanopy layers increase in weigth from the top of the tree. If under 1, they decrease (?) in weight. If set to zero, the specific leave weight remains contains throughout the canopy layer (and equal to SLWmax ?). Ameray et al. (2023) indicate in their parameter files that it’s hard to estimate, and that most studies use generic values.

Very surely

MaxLAI

No

Maximum leaf area index (LAI) that a species can achieve in its lifetime (for an age cohort ?).

This parameter is normally computed by the PnET model from other parameters. However, it can be specified for the species to control the early part of the species growth curve, as MaxLAI values inferior to the values calculated by the model allow cohorts to fill the canopy space faster (because the canopy space is based on the value calculated by the model ?). Still, the user guide recommends the MaxFracFol parameter to control the early part of the species growth curve rather than this one. Value must be decimal, superior or equal to 0.0. Units are in m2 of leaves / m2 of ground. Default is computed from other parameters.

Very surely

TOFol

Yes

Fraction of foliage biomass lost per year.

The reciprocal of leaf longevity. Value must be decimal, between 0.0 and 1.0. Units are proportion per year.

Maybe

TORoot

Yes

Fraction of root biomass lost per year.

Fraction of roots lost every year to damage, breakage or death. Value must be decimal, between 0.0 and 1.0. Units are proportion per year. Defaults to 0.02.

Maybe

TOWood

Yes

Fraction of wood biomass lost per year.

Fraction of wood (branches, stem, etc.) lost every year to damage, breakage or death. Value must be decimal, between 0.0 and 1.0. Units are proportion per year. Defaults to 0.01.

Maybe

AmaxA

Yes

Intercept of the relationship between foliar N and maximum net photosynthetic rate.

Is used to compute the Amax, the maximum photosynthetic rate (see Generic Parameters File). Value must be decimal, between -500 and +500. Units must be in nmol CO2g-1 leaf s-1.

Very surely

AmaxB

Yes

Slope of the relationship betwee foliar N and maximum net photosynthetic rate.

Is used to compute the Amax, the maximum photosynthetic rate (see Generic Parameters File). The equation is Amax (nmol CO2 g-1 leaf s-1) = AmaxA + AmaxB*FoliarN. Value must be decimal, superior to 0. Units are in nmol CO2g-1 leaf s-1.

Very surely

HalfSat

Yes

Half saturation light level for photosynthesis.

Light intensity at which the photosynthetic rate reaches half of its maximum value Lower values reflect more shade tolerance. Value must be integer superior to 0. However, the unit is up to the choice of the user : Must be either µmol/m2/sec or W/m2. Must be in the same unit as PARUnits (see Generic Parameters File) and PAR (see Climate Input File). The model cannot detect the unit, so be careful. Note that this is the only parameter in PnET that determines the shade tolerance of the species; the shade tolerance score in the LANDIS-II species parameter file is ignored by PnET Succession.

Yes

H1

Yes

Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).

Units : m of pressure head (pressure of water in the soil pores). H1 is the value of pressure head for which at which waterlogging is so important that photosynthesis cannot occur anymore, and hence the pressure at which the waterlogging stress is maximal for the species. H1 can take a negative value to allow for waterlogging tolerance (some photosynthesis activity despite a soil saturated beyond field capacity). Defaults to 0. Must be smaller that H2, H3 and H4. To recognize differences in waterloggin tolerance, using a trait database (such as TRY) is recommended by the user guide (see “Species tolerance to waterlogging” in TRY).

Yes

H2

Yes

Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).

Units : m of pressure head (pressure of water in the soil pores). H2 is the value of pressure head for which the species starts entering its ideal zone of soil water pressure for water absorption, and when the waterlogging stress stops. Often not known in reality. The user guide recommends using a value of 0.0 if no waterlogging effect need to be measured. Defaults to 3.64 (the pressure head often associated with soils at field capacity). Must be bigger than H1, but smaller than H3 and H4. To recognize differences in waterloggin tolerance, using a trait database (such as TRY) is recommended by the user guide (see “Species tolerance to waterlogging” in TRY).

Yes

H3

Yes

Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).

Units : m of pressure head (pressure of water in the soil pores). H3 corresponds to the value of pressure head after which trees start to experience water stress (lack of water causing a decrease in photosynthesis). Represents the drought sensivitity of the species. Must be higher than H1 and H2, smaller than H4. User guide recommends to have H3 = H4 (H4 being cessation of photosynthesis due to water stress) as most species don’t experience water stress until photosynthesis drops abruptly and ceases after a threshold. Defaults to 150. To recognize differences in drought tolerance, using a trait database (such as TRY) is recommended by the user guide (see “Species tolerance to drought” in TRY).

Yes

H4

Yes

Water stress parameter according to Feddes et al. (1978) (Fig. 59).

Units : m of pressure head (pressure of water in the soil pores). Must be bigger than H1, H2 and H3. H4 corresponds to the value of pressure head after photosynthesis completly ceases. User guide indicates that most litterature sources use a value of -153m, corresponding to a wilting point. Defaults to 150 in PnET succession. But to recognize differences in drought tolerance, using a trait database (such as TRY) is recommended by the user guide (see “Species tolerance to drought” in TRY).

Yes

PsnAgeRed

Yes

Reduction factor of photosynthesis as the age of an age cohort increases.

Gives the shape of the curve that links the value of fAge (the final reduction factor of photosynthesis based on age) and the age. Value must be decimal, superior to 0.0. Values inferior to 1 result in a rapid initial decline of photsynthesis with age; value of 1 makes the relation linear (gradual decline from age 1 to longevity of species); values above 1 result in a slow initial decline. Remember that the longevity is indicated in the LANDIS-II species file; but in the context of PnET, the user guide states that longevity should be “in optimal conditions” as several factors in the model will almost always kill age cohorts before their longevity. Cohorts die when the Non Structural (ressource) carbon in the cohort is less than 1% of the value of other biomass pools combined at the calendar year inside the model. Units are in proportion per year. Defaults to 5. Ameray et al. (2023) indicates in their parameter files that 3 seems to work well for all species in the boreal biome, allowing them to match the growth curves of Pothier and Savard (1998).

Very surely

LeafOnMinT

Yes

Minimum temperature for activate growing season.

Is compared to Tmin in the Climate Input File to determine the start and end month of the growing season in a year. Foliage is allocated during the first growing season month and some of it (see TOFol above) is dropped at the last month. Reducing LeafOnMinT lengthens the growing season. Value must be decimal, units are in °C. No default value provided.

Very surely

PsnTMin

Yes

Minimum average daytime temperature for photosynthesis.

Is compared to TDay in the Climate Input File. If TDay < PsnTMin, the temperature reduction factor for the photosynthesis will become 0, leading to no photosynthesis hapenning. Value is decimal, unit are in °C. No default value provided ?

Very surely

PsnTOpt

Yes

Optimal average daytime temperature for photosynthesis.

Is compared to TDay in the Climate Input File. If Tday = PsnTOpt, the temperature reduction factor for the photosynthesis will be 1.0 (no reduction). Value is decimal, superior to PsnTMin. Units are in °C.

Very surely

PsnTMax

No

Maximum average daytime temperature for photosynthesis.

Is compared to TDay in the Climate Input File. If TDay > PsnTMax, the temperature reduction factor for the photosynthesis will become 0, leading to no photosynthesis hapenning. Value is decimal, unit are in °C. Must be superior to PsnTOpt. If no value is provided, PsnTMax will be alculated as PsnTOpt + PsnTOpt – PsnTMin. Typically not greater than 37°C.

Very surely

ColTol

No

Cold tolerance (coldest temperature that the species can survive).

Age cohort is killed if TDay (see Climate Input File) < ColTol ? Cohorts can resprout after being killed by cold if their resprout probability is superior to 0. Value must be decimal, unit is °C. Defaults to -9999.

Very surely

k

Yes

Canopy light attenuation constant (light extinction coefficient).

Higher values produce more light attenuation for the leaves of the cohort for this species. Value must be decimal between 0.0 and 1.0. No units.

Very surely

DNSC

Yes

Proportion of Non Structural (ressources) carbon from the total activate biomass of the cohort that will be maintained as long as net photosynthesis exceeds maintenance respiration.

NSC is the first carbon pool of the tree to have carbon from photosynthesis allocated to it. After the proportion set up by DNSC is reached, carbon will start being allocated to other pools (?). Value must be decimal, between 0.0 and 1.0. Units are proportion of activate biomass. Default is 0.05.

Maybe

FracBelowG

Yes

Fraction of non-foliar biomass that is allocated belowground (root pool).

Allocations vary each year to maintain this fraction. Value must be decimal between 0.0 and 1.0. Unit is proportion. Default is 0.33.

Maybe

EstMoist

Yes

Scaling parameter that changes the maximum expected value of fWater when computing the probability of establishment.

Difficult to understand. According to the user guide, is typically used to make sure that the light and water available in a cell has equal effect on the computation of Pest. Value must be decimal between 0.0 and 1.0. Units are proportion. Default is 1.0.

No

EstRad

Yes

Scaling parameter that changes the maximum expected value of fRad when computing the probability of establishment.

Similar to EstMoist, but for light. See above. User guide recommends setting it to the maximum fRad value observed in the first year when a single cohort of a species is grown on an open site, as fRad is almost never equal to 1.0. Value must be decimal between 0.0 and 1.0. Units are proportion. Default is 1.0.

No

FracFol

Yes

Fraction of the active woody biomass (above or belowground) of a cohort that determines the amount of foliage maintained each year.

Value must be decimal, between 0.0 and 1.0. Units are proportion per year. Defaults to 0.033. One of the most important parameters in the sensitivity analysis of Ameray et al. (2023).

Maybe

FrActWd

Yes

Shape parameter of the negative exponential function that calculates the amount of woody biomass that has active xylem capable of supporting foliage.

All wood is “active” (capable of supporting foliage) when FrActWd = 0.0. Increasing values decrease the fraction of active wood as biomass increase. Value must be decimal between 0.0 and 0.4. No units. Default is 0.00004.

Maybe

CO2HalfSatEff

No

Slope coefficient used to reduce HalfSat (= increase shade tolerance) as CO2 concentration increase.

Value must be decimal, superior to 0.0. No unit. Set to 0 to turn off the effect. Defaults to 0.0 (no effect).

Maybe

CO2AMaxBEff

No

Proportional change in AmaxB (see above) at 550ppm CO2 relative to 350ppm.

Value must be decimal, superior to 0.0. Units in proportion. Omit or set to 1.0 to turn on the effect of CO2 concentration on photosynthesis through AMax (temperature).

Maybe

O3StomataSens

No

Species susceptibility of the stomata to ozone-induced sluggishness.

Value must be Sensitive, Intermediate, or Tolerant. The user guide doesn’t give more precisions as to how (quantitatively) this affects things in the model.

Yes

O3GrowthSens

No

Species susceptibility to ozone-induced tissue damage.

Scaling parameter; zero indicates no sensitivity, 2.0 indicates twice as much sensitivity as 1.0, 3.0 three times more, etc. Value must be decimal superior to 0.0. No units. Defaults to 0.0 (no effect).

Yes

MaxFolN

No

Controls the dynamic response of foliar nitrogen (FolN) to light.

Equation is AdjFolN = FolN + ((MaxFolN – FolN) * (fRad ^ FolNShape)). FolN reprsents the minimum bound for AdjFolN  (adjusted FolN). Value must be decimal, superior to 0. Unit is in %N per weight. Omit this parameter and FolNShape to turn off the effect. Defaults to -9999.

Very surely

FolNShape

No

Controls the dynamic response of foliar nitrogen (FolN) to light.

Equation is AdjFolN = FolN + ((MaxFolN – FolN) * (fRad ^ FolNShape)). FolN reprsents the minimum bound for AdjFolN (adjusted FolN). Value must be decimal, superior to 0. Unit is in %N per weight. Omit this parameter and MaxFolN to turn off the effect. Defaults to -0.0.

Very surely

MaxFracFol

No

Controls the dynamic response of the fraction of Foliage (FracFol) to light.

Equation is AdjFracFol = FracFol + ((MaxFracFol – FracFol) * (fRad^FracFolShape)). FracFol reprsents the minimum bound for AdjFracFol (adjusted FracFol). Value must be decimal, superior to 0. Unit is in proportion. Omit this parameter or set MaxFracFol equal to FracFol to turn off the effect. Defaults to -9999.

Very surely

FracFolShape

No

Controls the dynamic response of the fraction of Foliage (FracFol) to light.

Equation is AdjFracFol = FracFol + ((MaxFracFol – FracFol) * (fRad^FracFolShape)). FracFol reprsents the minimum bound for AdjFracFol (adjusted FracFol). Value must be decimal, superior to 0. No units. Omit this parameter or set MaxFracFol equal to FracFol to turn off the effect. Defaults to 0.0.

Very surely

LifeFrom

No

Specifies the life form (stature) of the species to determine the limits to the species major canopy layer.

In PnET Succession, the biomass of an age cohort is a proxy to its height. Therefore, the layers of the canopy (and their composition) are determined by cohort biomass. Tress can thus exist in any canopy layer. But some might want to simulate other species than trees, such as shrubs, that can only be in the lowest canopy layer. Value must be tree (can be in all canopy layers) or ground (will only be in lowest canopy layer). EDIT : Actually, section 11.6 of the user guide (about the Output Biomass PnET extension) reveal additional types (e.g. dark conifer, light conifer, etc.) that are needed for each species to properly compute albdo with Output Biomass PnET.

Yes

MossScalar

No

Unit converter from biomass to depth in the case of a pseudo species (moss).

PnET Succession can estimate the insulation value of a pseudo species when computing the soil temperature profile. In that case, this parameter allows the model compute the Depth of the insulation (in meter) as a product of MossScalar and the biomass of the species. Not needed if parameter SoilIceDepth is set to False (see Generic Parameters File). Set to 0 for species that are not soil-insulating groundcover species. Value must be decimal superior or equal to 0.0. Units are in meter. Defaults to 0.

No

Still waiting on an answer from Brian here to know what they have done.

Used to calibrate the reaction of the species to conditions of drought or waterlogging (see [7. Calibrating soil and water.ipynb](./7. Calibrating soil and water.ipynb)).

These traits are taken from the 

TRY database. See code above that generate a Python dictionnary with estimates of drought and waterlogging tolerance on a scale from 1 to 5.

from functionsForCalibration import *

records.content:
To create expected outcomes of competitions between species in conditions of increasing temperature, we need to have some idea of the either the tolerance of each tree species to higher temperature, or of the temperature they prefer to grow well.

Unfortunately, I wasn’t able to find any dataset regarding the temperature tolerance of best growth temperatures of tree species in Canada. While the TRY database have traits linked to temperature tolerance or preference, there seem to be relatively few measurements for Canadian tree species.

I’ve decided to go with an alternative route, and to compute estimates of the favorite growth temperature of species based on a simple assumption : where the tree species is most present/abundant in Canada, then the temperature is best for it. This is an imperfect assumption of course, as soils, disturbances and competition with other species also affect its presence; but I’ll consider it enough to produce reliable estimates that will give us a broad idea of the temperatures the species prefer.

Temperatures and precipitations are, according to the litterature, two of the most important factors explaining the distribution of tree species on earth. However, the effect of temperature on trees can appear in different ways, sometimes indirect; for example, by killing out insects or diseases that will attack the tree in other regions. As such, it’s not because a tree species is most abundant in a place with a given temperature that this means that this temperature is optimal for its growth : it might be that other effects of the temperatures (and also of precipitations) are at play to help the species grow in this place.

Here, we want to get a temperature for each tree species that reflects their relative “sweet spot” of growth (where their temperature is optimal for growth), which will help us predict the outcome of competition between our tree species in condtions where temperatures (and shade) are the only factors of competition. Ideally, we would get these through expeximentation, where we can put tree saplings in controlled conditions and with different temperatures in order to see how they respond to it. This would allow us to isolate the direct effect of temperature on tree growth, independant of other factors (i.e. disturbances, solar radiation, precipitations, etc.).

Unfortunatly, we do not have such experimentation data for tree species in Canada. But we do know that the direct effect of temperature on tree growth is one of the forces shapping the repartition of tree species in Canada. As such, I make the assumption that by looking at the average summer temperature accross Canada that we then average by ponderating it by the distribution of the biomass of the species accross Canada, we will get a value that is pretty close the optimal growth temperature of the species.

Tip

To read more about the limitations of this approach, and especially the lack of correlation between the localisation of a species and its optimal temperature, see 

here.

Also, you can read about the fact that while temperature as an effect on tree growth, in nature, this interact very closely with water availability, and that the notion of “optimal temperature” thus tends to change with the availability of water; see 

here.

So again, this approach has a lot of limitation; but I will still use it to try and catch any strange behavior from the model when species are competiting with each other in changing temperature conditions.

# Beforehand : importing packages + setting up the paths
# we are going to use
import numpy as np
import xarray as xr
import rasterio
import requests
from pathlib import Path
from tqdm import tqdm
import os
from pyproj import CRS
from rasterio.warp import reproject, Resampling
import json

# URL for the OpenDAP access to the climate data
# we will use to compute average summer temperatures
# URL taken from the THREDDS server of Ouranos
# See https://zenodo.org/records/17465777 for the URLS
# We choose the agregatted datasets (all years and variables in one dataset)
# at a monthly resolution for simulation with the MPI-ESM1-2-LR as the driver model of CRCM5.
# MPI-ESM1-2-LR has been recommanded as the best model for a calibration on historical data
# by Dominique Paquin, main author associated with these datasets.
climateDataURL = "https://pavics.ouranos.ca/twitcher/ows/proxy/thredds/dodsC/datasets/simulations/RCM-CMIP6/CORDEX/NAM-12/mon/NAM-12_MPI-ESM1-2-LR_historical_r1i1p1f1_OURANOS_CRCM5_v1-r1_mon_195001-201412.ncml"

# Path where we will save the raster with our summer temperature averaged
# This raster will have a pretty low resolution (0.11 arc degrees) and will cover Canada only,
# so it is very light.
exportPathSumerTempRaster = './ReferencesAndData/Climate Data/climate_summer_temp_latlon_Canada.tif'

# Path to the NFI raster for aboveground biomass
biomassNFIRasterPath = "./ReferencesAndData/NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Structure_Biomass_TotalLiveAboveGround_v1.tif"

# Path to the NFI rasters for the species abundance; SPECIES_NAME has to be replaced by the name of the species.
speciesNFIRasterPath = "./ReferencesAndData/NFI_Data/NFI_MODIS250m_2011_kNN_Species_SPECIES_NAME_v1.tif"

# List of species we are going to get the temperature for; 
# derived from the parameters loaded in 3.Initial_Species_Parameters.ipynb
with open("./SpeciesParametersSets/Initial/initialPnETSpeciesParameters.json", 'r') as source:
    speciesList = list(json.load(source)["PnETSpeciesParameters"].keys())
print("Species to be investigated : " + str(speciesList))

# We load the JSON dictionary in which we will save the average temperatures
# It's the dictionnary in which we saved TRY traits before
with open("./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerances.json", 'r') as file:
    speciesToleranceTraitsDict = json.load(file) 

# We load the JSON dictionary containing the equivalence between species full names and species code,
# which is needed at some point
with open("./ReferencesAndData/NFI_Data/NFI_SpeciesNameEquivalence.json", 'r') as file:
    speciesCodeDict = json.load(file) 
    # We reverse it because we will need the opposite
    speciesCodeDict_Inverted = {v: k for k, v in speciesCodeDict.items()}



print("\nStep 1: Loading Ouranos climate data with latitude subset...")
print("  Opening OPeNDAP dataset...")

ds = xr.open_dataset(climateDataURL)

# Get coordinate names
# Allows us to adapt to more formats of 
lon_name = 'lon' if 'lon' in ds.coords else 'longitude'
lat_name = 'lat' if 'lat' in ds.coords else 'latitude'

# Subset to latitudes >= 40°N【0】
# I do this because subsetting with a polygon that corresponds to Canada
# or to the extent of the NFI raster has proven to be quite the headache.
# This is simpler and it works very well.
print("  Subsetting to latitudes >= 40°N to reduce the data downloaded")
if ds[lat_name].ndim == 1:
    # 1D coordinates - simple selection
    ds_subset = ds.where(ds[lat_name] >= 40, drop=True)
else:
    # 2D coordinates - need to check any latitude value in row
    lat_mask = (ds[lat_name] >= 40).any(dim=ds[lat_name].dims[1])
    ds_subset = ds.isel({ds[lat_name].dims[0]: lat_mask})

print(f"  Subset dataset shape: {dict(ds_subset.dims)}")

# Filter for 1962-2011 and summer months
print("  Filtering temporal range (1962-2011) and summer months...")
ds_filtered = ds_subset.sel(time=slice('1962-01-01', '2011-12-31'))
summer_months = ds_filtered['time.month'].isin([6, 7, 8])
tas_summer = ds_filtered['tas'].isel(time=summer_months)

# Calculate mean summer temperature (convert K to °C)
print("  Computing mean summer temperature...")
mean_summer_temp = tas_summer.mean(dim='time') - 273.15

# Load into memory
print("  Loading climate data into memory...")
mean_summer_temp = mean_summer_temp.load()

print(f"  Climate data shape: {mean_summer_temp.shape}")
print(f"  Temperature range: {float(mean_summer_temp.min()):.2f}°C - {float(mean_summer_temp.max()):.2f}°C")







print("\nStep 2: Creating a raster from the average summer temperatures from the climate dataset...")

# Extract grid mapping attributes from the crs coordinate
# of the original dataset loaded with OpenDAP from Ouranos's servers
crs_var = ds['crs'].attrs
# print(crs_var)
# Create the rotated pole CRS
rotated_crs = CRS.from_cf(crs_var)

mean_summer_temp = mean_summer_temp.rio.set_spatial_dims(x_dim="rlon", y_dim="rlat", inplace=False)
mean_summer_temp = mean_summer_temp.rio.write_crs(rotated_crs, inplace=False)

# Reproject and save
mean_summer_temp_reprojected = mean_summer_temp.rio.reproject("epsg:4326")
mean_summer_temp_reprojected.rio.to_raster(exportPathSumerTempRaster)

print("The raster of average summer temperatures has been exported to " + str(exportPathSumerTempRaster))



print("\nStep 3: Resampling climate raster to NFI resolution...")

# Gathering metadata about dimension and resolution
# of the NFI rasters to which we want to resample
# climate data
with rasterio.open(biomassNFIRasterPath) as src:
    biomass = src.read(1).astype(np.float32)
    biomass_meta = src.meta.copy()
    biomass_transform = src.transform
    biomass_crs = src.crs

# Open climate raster
with rasterio.open(exportPathSumerTempRaster) as src_climate:
    # Use NFI raster dimensions and transform
    nfi_temp = np.zeros((biomass.shape[0], biomass.shape[1]), dtype=np.float32)

    print(f"  Reprojecting from {src_climate.crs} to {biomass_crs}...")
    print(f"  Target shape: {nfi_temp.shape}")

    # Reproject climate data to NFI grid
    reproject(
        source=rasterio.band(src_climate, 1),
        destination=nfi_temp,
        src_transform=src_climate.transform,
        src_crs=src_climate.crs,
        dst_transform=biomass_transform,
        dst_crs=biomass_crs,
        resampling=Resampling.bilinear
    )

print(f"  ✓ Reprojection complete")
print(f"  Resampled temperature range: {np.nanmin(nfi_temp):.2f}°C - {np.nanmax(nfi_temp):.2f}°C")
print(f"  Unique temperature values in resampled data: {len(np.unique(nfi_temp[~np.isnan(nfi_temp)]))}")

# Save resampled temperature raster
# temp_raster_path = './ReferencesAndData/Climate Data/summer_temperature_1962_2011_250m.tif'
# print(f"  Saving resampled temperature raster: {temp_raster_path.name}")
# with rasterio.open(temp_raster_path, 'w', **biomass_meta) as dst:
#     dst.write(nfi_temp, 1)
# print(f"  ✓ Resampled temperature raster saved")



# TRY TO MAKE THIS SECTION SMALLER
# And to re-use functions to download files that are already ready

print("\nStep 4: For each species, loading species abundance raster and computing biomass-ponderated average summer temperature")

print("Loading NFI aboveground biomass raster")
with rasterio.open(biomassNFIRasterPath) as src:
    # Handle nodata values for the NFI biomass raster (-3.4028235e+38)
    # These nodata values, if not indicated as nan, will mess up our computation later
    biomass_nodata = -3.4028235e+38
    print(f"  Biomass nodata value: {biomass_nodata}")
    biomass[biomass == biomass_nodata] = np.nan
    biomass[biomass < 0] = np.nan  # Additional safety for any negative values
    print(f"  Valid biomass cells: {np.sum(~np.isnan(biomass)):,}")


print("Loading NFI species raster and computing average temperature")
for species in speciesList:
    # The name of the species in the name of their respective NFI rasters is a bit different
    # than the NFI nomenclature (paradoxically) that we use in our parameter files.
    # We have to change it to get the right link.
    speciesNameForRasterPath = species.split(".")[0].capitalize() + "_" + species.split(".")[1].capitalize()
    speciesNFIRasterPath_Right = speciesNFIRasterPath.replace("SPECIES_NAME", speciesNameForRasterPath)

    # Loading the species abundance raster and dealing with its nodata values.
    with rasterio.open(speciesNFIRasterPath_Right) as src:
        species_pct = src.read(1).astype(np.float32)
        # Handle nodata values for the species raster (-9999)
        species_nodata = -9999
        # print(f"  Species nodata value: {species_nodata}")
        species_pct[species_pct == species_nodata] = np.nan
        species_pct[species_pct < 0] = np.nan  # Additional safety
        # print(f"  Valid species cells: {np.sum(~np.isnan(species_pct)):,}")

    # Calculate species biomass by multiply its abundance raster with the aboveground biomass raster
    print("  Computing species biomass...")
    species_biomass = (biomass * species_pct / 100.0).astype(np.float32)
    valid_biomass = species_biomass[~np.isnan(species_biomass) & (species_biomass > 0)]
    # if len(valid_biomass) > 0:
    #     print(f"  Species biomass range: {valid_biomass.min():.2f} - {valid_biomass.max():.2f} Mg")
    # else:
    #     print(f"  WARNING: No valid species biomass found!")

    # Mask invalid data (nan of infinite)
    valid_mask = (species_biomass > 0) & (~np.isnan(nfi_temp)) & (~np.isnan(species_biomass)) & (~np.isinf(species_biomass))
    print(f"  Valid cells for calculation: {valid_mask.sum():,}")
    
    if valid_mask.sum() == 0:
        print("\n  ERROR: No valid cells found for calculation!")
    else:
        # Vectorized weighted average
        weighted_sum = np.sum(nfi_temp[valid_mask] * species_biomass[valid_mask])
        total_biomass = np.sum(species_biomass[valid_mask])
        optimal_temp = weighted_sum / total_biomass
    
        print(f"\n{'='*60}")
        print(f"RESULTS FOR {species}")
        print(f"{'='*60}")
        print(f"Total species biomass: {total_biomass:,.2f} Mg")
        print(f"Number of cells with species: {valid_mask.sum():,}")
        print(f"Optimal summer temperature: {optimal_temp:.2f}°C")
        print(f"{'='*60}")

        # We save the temperature into the dictionnary
        # We have to detected the right species
        speciesToleranceTraitsDict[speciesCodeDict_Inverted[species]]["Estimated optimal summer temperature"] = optimal_temp

        # We delete the unused arrays
        del species_pct

# We save and display the dictionnary
print(speciesToleranceTraitsDict)
with open("./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerances.json", 'w') as file:
    json.dump(speciesToleranceTraitsDict, file, indent=4)

records.content:
After the first calibration step (where we simulate an ideal situation for each tree species with no competition, good soil and enough precipitations for water, etc.), we will do several more step where we will check if the model produces “realistic” competition outcomes of species against species in several conditions :

Competition for shade (two species together with a good water input through soil and precipitation)

Drought (two species together but with a lack of water)

Waterlogging (two species together but too much water)

Temperature (two species together with increased temperatures)

This is inspired by this text from the 

calibration tips of Eric Gustafson :

It is prudent to test the competitive behavior of pairs of species to ensure that the behavior is appropriate. I recommend that you conduct some tests of two and/or three species combinations using your calibration setup to ensure that species compete as you would expect. Extremely poor competitive ability usually results only when a cohort is inappropriately overtopped by another and becomes lightstarved. (...) It is highly recommended that you verify your calibrations by simulating several similar species together to ensure that they in fact compete as expected. If not, often a slight tweak of one parameter can bring them in line with expectations.

Ideally, we will test every species against another, and see which one “wins” by having the most biomass in the pixel after a long time (100 years), and compare this against “expected outcomes”. Expected outcomes are predictions we will make in advance based on what we know of the biology of each tree species. For example, if we put a shade tolerant species with a shade intolerant species in competition in otherwise ideal conditions of water and temperature, we expect the shade tolerant species to “win” on the long term. If the model cannot produce the expected outcomes, that might mean that we haven’t represented the differences between species in the right way (e.g., their relative difference in shade tolerance is not implemented well enough in our parameters). But getting these inter-species different is crucial according to 

Eric Gustafson so that the model can properly represent the competition outcomes between them.

But it’s not that simple. Here, the challenge comes in defining the expected outcomes (how do we know which species is going to win ?) and what “winning” means.

Defining a win is difficult because of the variability that comes with it. E.g., a shade tolerant species versus a shade intolerant one might reach more biomass than the other after 100 years; but maybe not if the cohorts starts at very different age, or with very different biomass. For example, the competiton could start with a very large and old shade intolerant cohort, which will take the shade tolerant species a long time to overcome.

Defining the expected outcomes (who wins) is also difficult because we have no solid empirical data on the competition between species; or at least, not enough empirical data to quantity how easy or how frequent it will be to win for a species against another. How do we know if Acer rubrum will tend to “win” against Abies balsamea in a competition for light one 100 years of simulation ? It’s doubly difficult because empirical data comes from a place where many competitions and factor affect tree growth at once (shade, water, temperature, etc.), along with disturbances (which we’re not including here).

As such, the best we can do here is to use arbitrarely-defined expected outcomes (although based on the real and empirical/know difference in shade tolerance, drought tolerance, etc...of our species), and use arbitrarely-defined definitions of a “win” and of a threshold above which we consider that there is too much difference between the expected outcome of the actual ouctomes of the competition from the simulations.

To define the expected outcomes, I propose to use the tolerance traits that can be found in the 

TRY database : Shade tolerance, drought tolerance, waterlogging tolerance, as well as the optimal temperature we calculated in a previous section. These traits are often used in other LANDIS-II studies (see supp. material from 

Reese et al. 2024) to calibrate PnET-Succession of produce expected outcomes.

Here is the arbitrary methodology that I propose to use :

We define a “win” when one species has achieved more biomass than the other after 100 years of simulation.

To define the intensity or the variation in “winning” for one species competing against another, we will make many simulations where we start with cohorts of different ages for the two species. We will compute the amount of cells in which, after 100 years of simulation, the “challenger” species has more biomass than the “opponent” species. This amount will correspond to the intensity of “wins”. For example, if 60% of cells have more biomass for the challenger species after 100 years of simulation, then we will say that it has a win rate of 60%.

To define the expected outcomes, we will do a simple computation based on the tolerance traits, but always account for the competition for shade (which is still there despite the inclusion of drought, waterlogging or changing temperatures)

For shade, the expected win will be derived linearly from the following rules :

If the challenger species has a tolerance of 5 (very tolerant) and the opponent has a tolerance of 1 (very intolerant), then we expect a 100% winning frequency for the challenger.

If the oppososite happens (1 for the challenger, 5 for the opponent), we expect a 0% winning frequency.

If the challenger and the oponnent have the same shade tolerance, we expect a 50% winning frequency.

For any other combination of shade tolerance between challenger and opponent, we derive the winning frequency linearly from the previous rules (prob_win = 0.5 + 0.125 * diff)

For drought and waterlogging, the rules are as follow :

Simulations for drought will be done with an excessively drained soil that doesn’t retain water much (e.g. coarse sand), as per the supp. material A from 

Reese et al. 2024

Simulatons for waterlogging will be done with a soil that retains water a lot (e.g.. mucky peat), as per supp. material A from 

Reese et al. 2024

We first compute the frequency of winning with the same rule à with the shade tolerance (see above), but with the drought tolerance or waterlogging tolerance used in the calculation instead.

Then, we also compute the frequency of winning the shade competition (based on the shade tolerance) with the same rule as before.

We then compute the final expected frequency of winning the competition by doing a ponderated average of the expected frequency of winning the shade competition, and the expected frequency of winning the drought or waterlogging competition. In that way, we still account for the shade competition happenning.

For temperatures, the rules are as follow :

If the challenger species has an optimal temperature (as calculated in a previous section) that is 5 degrees celcius or more closer to the average summer temperature that will be use in the competition simulation when compared to the opponent species, then it has 100% change winning. For example : if the average summer temperature in the simulation is 12 degrees celcius, and the optimal temperature of the challenge is 11 degrees while the one of the opponent is 6 degrees celcius, then the challenger species has an optimal temperature 5 degrees closers to the average summer temperature in the simulation, and is therefore predicted to have 100% change winning.

If the opposite happens, then we expect a 0% winning frequency for the challenger.

We then compute the final expected frequency of winning the competition by doing a ponderated average of the expected frequency of winning the temperature competition, and the expected frequency of winning the drought or waterlogging competition. In that way, we still account for the shade competition happenning.

Here is some code that reads these probabilities from the TRY database, and then computes the expected frequencies of winning and displays them in a plot :

from functionsForCalibration import *

# dictionnaryOfTraits = extract_try_traits("./ReferencesAndData/TRY data/46003.txt")
# # We remove tolerance to fire; not useful for us.
# for species in dictionnaryOfTraits.keys():
#     del dictionnaryOfTraits[species]["Species tolerance to fire"]
# print(dictionnaryOfTraits)

with open("./ReferencesAndData/Functional Traits/SpeciesTolerances.json", 'r') as file:
    dictionnaryOfTraits = json.load(file) 
# We remove tolerance to fire; not useful for us.
for species in dictionnaryOfTraits.keys():
    del dictionnaryOfTraits[species]["Species tolerance to fire"]

# # We create average values of tolerance accross the different measure of TRY,
# # using some simple and arbitrary rules to translate the different measurement systems
# # into one. See average_tolerance_traits() in functionsForCalibration.py
# averageTraitTol = average_tolerance_traits(dictionnaryOfTraits)
# print(averageTraitTol)

# We compute and plot the expected outcomes of the competition
# between each species. Rules are in plot_competition_matrices_with_shade() in
# functionsForCalibration.py
competitionDictionnary = plot_competition_matrices_with_shade(dictionnaryOfTraits)

# We export the dictionnary with the predicted probabilities to a JSON file
import json, os
if not os.path.exists("./ReferencesAndData/CompetitionOutcomes/"):
    os.mkdir("./ReferencesAndData/CompetitionOutcomes/")
with open('./ReferencesAndData/CompetitionOutcomes/competitionDictionnary.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(competitionDictionnary, f)

Now that we have initial parameters for our species, we need to define the landscape in which the calibration will take place.

To adapt to the Python functions I’ve made for these Jupyter Notebooks (see 

here), simulations will take place on a single cell.

What we need is :

The climate file (containing all of the climate variables need by PnET)

The ecoregion file and PnET ecoregion parameters (to decide which ones will be used during the calibration)

Initial communities files (will be created on the spot during the calibration depending on what we need)

records.content:
See 

Gustafson and Miranda (2023) for detailed information about what the climate file used in PnET simulations must contain.

Gustafson and Miranda (2023) recommends using a constant climate for calibrating (p. 69). Gustafson also recommends using an “ideal” climate at some points, but deciding what is an ideal climate for a given species is a difficult proposition. As we are going to compare the growth curves generated by PnET Succession with the growth curves estimated from data of the National Forest Inventory (NFI) of Canada (see 

​.​/5​.Other​_important​_data​_before​_starting​.ipynb), the idea will be rather to get long-term monthly averages (as recommanded by 

Gustafson and Miranda (2023) p. 73) in the study landscape.

PnET Succession requires 5 climate variables to function :

Maximum Monthly temperature (°C)

Minimum Monthly temperature (°C)

Photosynthetically Active Radiation (umol/m2/s)

Sum of precipitations during the month (mm)

Mean monthly atmospheric CO2 concentration (ppm)

Making a climate file for PnET-Succession, or for the Climate Library of LANDIS-II ?

There are two ways of providing climate data to PnET-Succession :

Either with a climate file that is read directly by PnET-Succession (with the internal parsing code of PnET-Succession). See 

the user guide of PnET-Succession, section 6 to see what the file must look like.

With the dedicated “Climate library” of LANDIS-II. The climate library is made so that users can provide a single climate data file for several extensions of LANDIS-II. To use it with PnET-Succession, you must make a climate file (or even 2 if you want to make a separate file for the spinup climate data) according to the syntax highlighted in the 

user guide of the climate library. Then, you must make a “Climate config file” to indicate to the climate library where to find the climate files, and how to interpret them (there are several options indicated in the user guide; for example, you can use the average values of each months, etc. See the user guide for more). For an example of this climate config file, see 

ClimateConfigSimpleSims​.txt. Then, you just have to give the location of this config file to PnET-Succession through the parameter ClimateConfigFile in the main parameter file of PnET-Succession.

Here, we will be making a climate file to be used with the climate library of LANDIS-II. I think that this is the approach most recommanded by the devellopers, and it’s also better on the long term so that you can re-use this scripts to make parameter files for your simulations with other LANDIS-II extensions.

records.content:
Our study landscape for this calibration (see 

0​.2​.Goals​_and​_assumptions​.ipynb) is located in Quebec, in the Mauricie region.

We will thus get climate data for this area. Since this is a large area, it will certainly cover several cells from the climate datasets we are going to use (see below). In addition, this area landscape two zones with a relatively different climate : warmer and more mixed forests in the south, and colder and more boreal forests in the north of the area.

In order to simplify things for this calibration, the climate stream we will use for our simulation will be the one of the climate cell closest to the center (centroid) of the study landscape. Therefore, the climate stream that we will generate here will not be averaged accoss space. But we will average it accross time (see below) in order to create “mild” and “ideal” conditions without extreme events, by averaging every day of the year accross a the historical period for which we have the data (i.e. every january 1st data for a given variable will the same, and will be the average of all january 1st in the original).

records.content:
I did a lot of searching for a climate dataset that would be easy to access to make historical climate files for this calibration, but which would also be useful for “real” simulations once the calibration was done.

At first, I looked into the 

ESGF Metagrid, which is (to my understanding) an enormous decentralized catalog of climate data (data generated by Global Climate Models by different research time throughout the world). There is an enormous trove of data here, and it can be accessed quite easily using the 

intake-esgf Python package. However, one needs to search in this catalog to find datasets with historical data, the right variables, the right time step, etc. It was quite hard, and not always very stable; this is because the datasets are hosted on “nodes” run by several universities throughout the world, and these would sometime shut down or be unavailable for a time.

Then, a dataset was very recently published (at the time of writing this), in December 2025, by Ouranos - a Quebec-based research consortium on climate which regularly produces climate dataset for Canada or for all of North america. This dataset, which had a higher resolution that most others, seemed perfect for our use.

Here, I describe the dataset from Ouranos; 

see here for the article describing the dataset.

All variables we need for PnET-Succession are available in the dataset (see 

this pdf from the 

Zenodo webpage of the dataset for the variable list) :

tasmax => Daily or monthly

tasmin => Daily or monthly

rsds (downwelling shortwave radiation) => 1 hour or daily or monthly

pr (precipitations, liquid or solid !) => 1 hour or daily daily or monthly

The dataset also seem to contain all of the variables needed for BFOLDS succession (wind speed and direction, relative humidity, etc.).

records.content:
Resolution : 0.11 degrees, about 12 km resolution, timestep of 5 minutes inside the model.

Simulations are produced at 0.11° horizontal grid spacing over North America from 1950 to 2100.

Up to four different emissions scenarios are used for future climate simulations (2015–2100).

Thefore, historical data is for 1950-2015.

Here, we will use Monthly data, as we don’t need to go at a daily basis.
